title: The Divinity Verses Examined One by One
subtitle: John 1:1, 8:58, 10:30, 20:28, Philippians 2:6-8, Colossians 2:9, and Titus 2:13 — the Greek grammar, the Trinitarian case, and the unitarian case, side by side
summary: The case that the New Testament calls Jesus God rests on a small set of frequently cited passages. This article examines the seven most important one by one — John 1:1, John 8:58, John 10:30, John 20:28, Philippians 2:6-8, Colossians 2:9, and Titus 2:13 — giving the precise Greek grammatical issue at stake in each, the mainstream Trinitarian reading with its named scholarly defenders, and the unitarian or Muslim reading with its own, without declaring a winner. It closes by examining how each tradition weighs these texts against Jesus's own statements of subordination to the Father.

seo.metaTitle: The Divinity Verses Examined One by One: John 1:1 to Titus 2:13
seo.metaDescription: A verse-by-verse, Greek-grammar-level examination of the seven New Testament passages most cited for Jesus's divinity, with the Trinitarian and unitarian readings presented at full strength and no verdict declared.
keywords: John 10:30 I and the Father are one
Thomas my Lord and my God John 20:28

sections.title: In Brief
sections.body:
Seven New Testament passages carry most of the weight in the historic Christian case that Jesus is God: John 1:1, John 8:58, John 10:30, John 20:28, Philippians 2:6–8, Colossians 2:9, and Titus 2:13. This article takes each one in turn, at the level of the underlying Greek, and lays out two things side by side: the mainstream Trinitarian reading, with its named scholarly defenders, and the unitarian or Muslim reading, with its own named defenders.

Neither reading is presented here as self-evidently correct. Some of the grammatical questions involved — the anarthrous “theos” of John 1:1, the article-sharing construction of Titus 2:13, the rare word harpagmos in Philippians 2:6 — are genuinely disputed among specialists who hold no personal stake in the theological outcome; others are not disputed at all, and this article says so plainly where that is the case. It closes not with a tally of verses but with the deeper, systematic question underneath all seven passages: how each tradition decides which texts control the reading of the others, given that the same Gospels also record Jesus calling the Father “greater than I” and disclaiming knowledge of “that day and hour.”

sections.title: How to Read What Follows
sections.body:
This article follows a stricter method than a typical comparative essay, because the questions it examines are not primarily about theology in the abstract but about how specific Greek sentences function grammatically — questions that can, in many cases, be checked against the wider corpus of Koine Greek rather than argued from doctrine alone.

For each of the seven passages, four things are given: the verse itself, quoted from the World English Bible; the precise grammatical or lexical issue at stake in the underlying Greek text; the Trinitarian reading, attributed to named scholars and standard reference works; and the unitarian or Muslim reading, likewise attributed by name. Where a reading is a minority position among specialists — as with the “exclamation” reading of John 20:28, or the fully indefinite “a god” reading of John 1:1 — this article says so explicitly, because presenting a minority view without noting its minority status would misrepresent the state of the scholarship. Where the grammar is genuinely close to a coin flip among neutral specialists — as with the theological implications of the qualitative reading at John 1:1, or the sense of harpagmos in Philippians 2:6 — this article says that too.

No passage below is used to declare which Christology is correct. That question depends on prior commitments — about whether the New Testament’s highest language about Jesus should govern the reading of his statements of subordination, or the reverse — that the grammar of any single verse cannot settle by itself. The closing section returns to that systematic point directly.

sections.title: John 1:1 — “The Word Was God,” or “What God Was, the Word Was”?
sections.body:
John’s Gospel opens: “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.” (John 1:1, World English Bible)

The Greek of the third clause reads: kai theos ēn ho logos — literally, “and God was the Word.” Two features of this clause drive the entire debate. First, word order: Greek is far more flexible than English about where a sentence’s subject and predicate fall, and here the predicate noun theos (“God”) stands before the verb ēn (“was”), while the subject ho logos (“the Word”) — marked as the subject by its article ho — follows the verb. Second, and more consequential: theos in this clause has no article. Earlier in the same verse, “the Word was with God” uses ton theon, with the article, referring to God the Father. In the very next clause, theos appears without one.

Mainstream Christian translations — the WEB quoted above, the KJV, NIV, ESV, NASB — render the clause “the Word was God,” taking the anarthrous theos as asserting full deity of the same kind possessed by “God” (the Father) named a moment earlier, while the distinct persons remain grammatically distinguished (the Word was with God, and was God). This reading has a long grammatical defense behind it. In 1933, E. C. Colwell published “A Definite Rule for the Use of the Article in the Greek New Testament” (Journal of Biblical Literature 52), observing that definite predicate nouns preceding the verb in Greek usually appear without the article. Applied to John 1:1c, the reasoning runs: theos here is anarthrous because it precedes the verb, not because it is indefinite — so the missing article does not, by itself, mean “a god” rather than “God.”

Colwell’s own rule, however, is narrower than it is sometimes made to sound, a point conceded across the theological spectrum. Colwell was describing definite nouns already known to be definite from context elsewhere; his statistics do not independently prove that theos in John 1:1c is definite in the first place — inferring definiteness backward from anarthrousness and preverbal position reverses the direction of his own argument. This limitation is now widely acknowledged among Greek grammarians regardless of their theological conclusions about the verse.

That gap was addressed differently by Philip B. Harner, whose 1973 article “Qualitative Anarthrous Predicate Nouns: Mark 15:39 and John 1:1” (Journal of Biblical Literature 92) surveyed anarthrous predicate nouns preceding the verb throughout the New Testament and found that a large majority function neither as definite nor as indefinite nouns, but qualitatively — describing the nature or character of the subject rather than its numerical identity. Harner concluded that John 1:1c is “primarily qualitative in meaning,” asserting that the Word has the nature of theos rather than identifying the Word as one particular, definite being. He pointed to the New English Bible’s 1970 rendering as capturing this sense: “When all things began, the Word already was. The Word dwelt with God, and what God was, the Word was.”

The two readings then diverge over what the qualitative sense implies theologically, and this is where genuine, ongoing disagreement lies rather than settled grammar. Mainstream Trinitarian scholars — Murray J. Harris (Jesus as God, 1992), D. A. Carson, and others — read the qualitative sense as affirming that the Word possesses the Father’s full divine nature, precisely the point later formalized at Nicaea as homoousios (“of the same essence”): not a lesser or derivative “godlikeness,” but the very nature of God, in a distinct person. On this reading, the qualitative sense is not an alternative to “the Word was God” — it is the fullest possible statement of it.

Unitarian and Muslim readers of the same qualitative evidence draw a different theological conclusion. If theos here describes quality or nature rather than asserting numerical identity with “God” named in the previous clause, the verse can be read as saying the Word possessed a nature that is godlike, divine, or God-derived in character, without asserting that the Word is personally, ontologically identical to the one God to whom Jesus elsewhere prays and whom he calls “the only true God” (John 17:3). On this reading, John is doing with the Logos something comparable to what Jewish Wisdom literature had already done with personified Wisdom (Proverbs 8) — describing an agent who reveals and expresses God’s nature and speech without being a co-equal member of the Godhead. The furthest end of this spectrum is the New World Translation’s “the Word was a god,” treating theos as fully indefinite — a rendering most grammarians, Trinitarian and non-Trinitarian alike, regard as a weak minority position, since a monotheistic Jewish author is unlikely to assert the existence of “a god” beside God without qualification.

What can be said plainly is this: the claim that Colwell’s rule alone proves “the Word was God” in the full Trinitarian sense is not accepted by careful grammarians today, on any side; the qualitative reading defended by Harner and reflected in the NEB is accepted by scholars across the theological spectrum as grammatically sound; and what that qualitative reading means theologically — full co-equal deity, or an exalted but distinct divine agent — is a live and unresolved disagreement that the Greek grammar of this one clause, by itself, does not settle.

sections.title: John 8:58 and John 9:9 — Divine Name, or Ordinary Self-Identification?
sections.body:
Jesus tells his opponents: “Jesus said to them, “Most certainly, I tell you, before Abraham came into existence, I AM.”” (John 8:58, World English Bible) The Greek behind the final two words is prin Abraam genesthai egō eimi — “before Abraham came-to-be, I am.” The crowd’s reaction follows immediately: they pick up stones to kill him (John 8:59).

Mainstream Christian scholarship — including commentators such as Raymond E. Brown, C. K. Barrett, and D. A. Carson — reads the absolute egō eimi (with no predicate noun following it, unlike “I am the bread of life” or “I am the good shepherd” elsewhere in John) as a deliberate echo of God’s self-identification to Moses at the burning bush, rendered in the Greek Septuagint of Exodus 3:14 as egō eimi ho ōn (“I am the one who is”), and of the recurring divine self-identification “I am he” in the Greek text of Isaiah 40–55. On this reading, Jesus is not merely claiming to predate Abraham in age; he is applying to himself the divine self-naming formula, and the crowd’s attempt to stone him — the penalty for blasphemy — is read as evidence they heard it that way.

The unitarian and Muslim response begins with a plain textual observation: egō eimi is ordinary Koine Greek for self-identification, used throughout the New Testament by people making no claim to deity at all — by Judas (Matthew 26:25), by the angel Gabriel (Luke 1:19), by Peter (Acts 10:21), by Paul (Acts 22:3). The clearest parallel sits only one chapter later. In John 9, a man born blind is healed; his neighbors debate whether he is really the same man, and: “Others were saying, “It is he.” Still others were saying, “He looks like him.” He said, “I am he.”” (John 9:9, World English Bible) The Greek behind “I am he” is the identical egō eimi. No reader takes the healed beggar to be claiming the divine name; most English translations, including the WEB quoted here, render his words in ordinary lower case as “I am he,” while rendering the same two Greek words at 8:58 as “I AM,” capitalized as a divine title. That capitalization is an interpretive choice made in English, not something inherent in the Greek itself — a point unitarian writers, for example at BiblicalUnitarian.com and in the broader tradition associated with Anthony Buzzard, press directly.

A further reading, distinct from the ordinary-self-identification argument, addresses what Jesus is claiming to precede: not necessarily his own personal existence before Abraham’s birth, but his precedence within God’s plan. The verse immediately before has Jesus say, “Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day. He saw it, and was glad” (John 8:56). On this “predestination” reading, favored in unitarian and some Socinian-tradition commentary, “before Abraham came into existence, I am” continues that thought: Jesus, as the one appointed from before Abraham within God’s redemptive purpose (compare 1 Peter 1:20, of Christ “foreknown indeed before the foundation of the world”), already held that place in God’s plan when Abraham looked forward to it — a claim about foreordained precedence and status, not a claim of personal pre-existence as a divine being.

The stoning in verse 59 is read by mainstream scholars as corroborating evidence of a blasphemy charge, but it is worth noting, in fairness to the unitarian response, that — unlike John 10:33, where the crowd states its charge explicitly (“because you, being a man, make yourself God”) — John 8:59 records the crowd’s action without recording their stated reason. Whether they reacted to a perceived claim to the divine name specifically, or to what could be heard as an audacious claim of priority and authority over their father Abraham, is left for the reader to infer, and mainstream and unitarian scholars draw that inference differently.

sections.title: John 10:30 and John 17:21–22 — One Being, or One in Will?
sections.body:
Jesus says to a hostile crowd: “I and the Father are one.” (John 10:30, World English Bible) The Greek is hen esmen, using the neuter numeral hen (“one thing”) rather than the masculine heis (“one person”) — a grammatical detail both sides of the debate treat as significant, though they draw opposite conclusions from it.

Mainstream Trinitarian scholars, including D. A. Carson and Andreas Köstenberger in their commentaries on John, note that the neuter hen rules out modalism (the Father and Son are not one and the same person) while still asserting a unity deep enough to justify the crowd’s reaction two verses later: “The Jews answered him, “We don’t stone you for a good work, but for blasphemy: because you, being a man, make yourself God.”” (John 10:33, World English Bible) On this reading, the immediate narrative context — the crowd hearing a claim to deity, and Jesus’s own preceding statement that he and the Father together, with equal power, keep his sheep safe (John 10:28–29) — indicates that the “oneness” of verse 30 includes shared divine power and nature, not merely agreement of purpose.

The unitarian and Muslim reading takes the neuter hen in the opposite direction: “one thing,” grammatically, most naturally describes a unity of will, purpose, or action rather than a unity of being or essence — the kind of oneness two distinct persons can share without being numerically or ontologically one entity. The strongest textual support for this reading comes from Jesus’s own words later in the same Gospel, in his prayer for his disciples: “that they may all be one; even as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be one in us; that the world may believe that you sent me. The glory which you have given me, I have given to them; that they may be one, even as we are one;” (John 17:21-22, World English Bible) Jesus asks that his disciples share the very same oneness — using the same vocabulary of mutual indwelling — that he shares with the Father. Since no one holds that Jesus is praying for his disciples to merge into a single divine essence with God, the parallel suggests, on the unitarian reading, that the “oneness” of both passages is relational and purposive rather than metaphysical.

Mainstream commentators respond that the prayer of John 17 functions by analogy rather than full equivalence: the disciples’ unity is patterned after, and made possible by, the Father-Son unity, without the passage claiming the two unities are identical in kind — the word “as” (kathōs) introduces a model to imitate, Carson argues, not a strict equation of natures. Other scholars, such as Raymond Brown, read John 10:30 as combining both dimensions at once for the Fourth Gospel: functional unity of mission and power, grounded in and pointing toward a claim about shared nature that the crowd’s own accusation of blasphemy makes explicit.

That accusation itself is met, in the text, not with a denial but with an argument: Jesus replies by quoting Psalm 82:6, “I said, you are gods,” originally addressed to human judges who received God’s word (John 10:34-36). Scholarly opinion divides sharply on how to weigh this reply. Carson and other mainstream commentators read it as an argument from the lesser to the greater: if Scripture itself can apply the word “gods” to mere human recipients of God’s word without blasphemy, then Jesus — sanctified and sent by the Father in a unique sense — has still greater warrant for language of divine sonship, so the citation strengthens rather than undercuts his claim. Unitarian readers take the same citation as evidence that “god”-language in Scripture already operates in a derivative, functional sense applied to humans, and read Jesus’s appeal to that precedent as clarifying, rather than escalating, what he had claimed — a deflection of a charge of literal deity by appeal to Scripture’s own looser use of the term. Both readings are represented in mainstream Johannine scholarship, and the passage itself does not adjudicate between them.

sections.title: John 20:28 — Thomas’s Confession, Read Two Ways
sections.body:
After the resurrection, the disciple Thomas, having doubted the other disciples’ report, is invited by the risen Jesus to touch his wounds. The Gospel records: “Thomas answered him, “My Lord and my God!”” (John 20:28, World English Bible) The Greek — ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou — places both nouns under their own article, addressed, according to the narrative, directly to Jesus (“answered him”).

Mainstream Johannine scholarship — Raymond Brown, D. A. Carson, Richard Bauckham, and N. T. Wright among them — treats this as the Fourth Gospel’s climactic Christological confession. It forms a deliberate frame with the Prologue’s “the Word was God” (John 1:1), and it anticipates the Gospel’s own stated purpose two verses later: “these are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God” (John 20:31). On this reading, the confession is addressed to Jesus in the flow of direct dialogue — Jesus responds to it in the next verse (“Have you believed because you have seen me?”) rather than correcting it — which is read as tacit acceptance of, rather than objection to, being called theos.

A minority position, held by some unitarian writers and a small number of historical-critical scholars, proposes that Thomas’s words function as an exclamation of astonishment — comparable to a modern speaker crying “my God!” in shock — rather than a considered doctrinal address identifying Jesus with God the Father. A related “representative” reading, associated with older Socinian-tradition commentary and occasionally revived in modern unitarian apologetics, holds that “my Lord” addresses the risen Jesus while “my God” is a doxological aside directed to the Father, whose power is recognized as manifest in the resurrection. It should be stated plainly, in fairness to the state of the scholarship, that this exclamation or representative reading is a minority position among Johannine scholars: most commentators, on both confessional and purely historical-critical grounds, read the narrative grammar (“answered him”) as making Thomas’s words a direct address to Jesus within the dialogue, not an aside to someone else in the room.

The strongest counterweight to the mainstream reading comes not from disputing the grammar of 20:28 itself but from placing it beside other statements in the very same Gospel, within a few verses. Only eleven verses earlier, the newly risen Jesus tells Mary Magdalene: “Jesus said to her, “Don’t hold me, for I haven’t yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.’”” (John 20:17, World English Bible) And in his prayer the night before his death, Jesus defines eternal life this way: “This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.” (John 17:3, World English Bible) Both statements come from Jesus himself, in John, and both distinguish him from “the only true God” and give him, too, a God of his own to worship. Mainstream Christian theology reconciles all three texts through the doctrine of Christ’s two natures: as God, he receives Thomas’s worship; as man, he has a God and prays to one. Unitarian and Muslim readers regard 17:3 and 20:17 as Jesus’s own plain self-definition, and treat the two-natures explanation as a framework required to hold the texts together rather than one demanded by any of the three verses read on its own.

sections.title: Philippians 2:6-8 — Something Not Grasped, or Not Held?
sections.body:
Paul writes of Christ: “who, existing in the form of God, didn’t consider equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself, becoming obedient to death, yes, the death of the cross.” (Philippians 2:6-8, World English Bible)

The pivotal word is harpagmos, translated above as “a thing to be grasped.” It occurs nowhere else in the entire New Testament, and its precise sense — built from the verb harpazō, “to seize” or “to plunder” — has been debated by classicists and New Testament scholars for well over a century.

The most influential modern treatment favoring the traditional kenotic reading is N. T. Wright’s 1986 essay, “Harpagmos and the Meaning of Philippians 2:5–11” (Journal of Theological Studies 37), building on earlier philological work by R. W. Hoover. Wright argues that harpagmos here means something closer to “a thing to be used for one’s own advantage” than “a thing to be seized” — the point of the sentence being that Christ, who already possessed equality with God (existing “in the form of God” prior to anything described in the following clauses), did not treat that possession as leverage to exploit, but instead chose to empty himself. On this reading, the passage presupposes Christ’s pre-existent deity as the premise for the humility it praises: he had equality with God, and did not use it for his own advantage — a deliberate contrast with Adam, made in God’s image, who grasped at “being like God” (Genesis 3:5) and fell. Kenotic Christology, developed further by theologians from the nineteenth century onward, reads this passage as the New Testament’s most direct statement of the incarnation as a voluntary act of divine self-limitation.

A different reading, argued at length by James D. G. Dunn in Christology in the Making (1980; 2nd ed. 1989), holds that the passage is best read through an “Adam Christology”: the correspondences between the hymn and the Genesis narrative — the “form”/“image” of God, a temptation toward grasping equality with God, obedience contrasted with disobedience, exaltation as the consequence of the choice made — suggest that the subject in view is Christ considered under the categories of Adam and humanity generally, not a pre-existent divine being descending into flesh. On Dunn’s reading, the “emptying” and “humbling” describe the free moral choice of the man Jesus, who, unlike Adam, did not grasp at God-likeness, and who was consequently exalted as the “last Adam” (compare 1 Corinthians 15:45, Romans 5:14). If Dunn is right, “existing in the form of God” need not presuppose personal pre-existence at all — the premise Wright’s reading requires is, on this view, not demanded by the text itself. Some Muslim and unitarian writers draw on this Adam-Christology reading to argue that Philippians 2 describes the moral choice of an exalted human being rather than the humility of an incarnate divine person.

Dunn’s reading, while taken seriously within mainstream academic New Testament scholarship and influential in its own right, remains a minority position relative to the majority of commentators — including many with no confessional stake in the outcome — who continue to read “existing in the form of God” and “emptied himself” as most naturally describing a transition from a genuinely prior mode of existence into human form. It is also worth noting that even among scholars who accept a pre-existence reading, there is a live and separate debate about what “emptied himself” costs the Son: some nineteenth-century kenotic theologians argued Christ actually laid aside the exercise, or even the possession, of certain divine attributes during his earthly life; classical Chalcedonian orthodoxy denies this is possible, holding that the divine nature cannot change, and that it is the mode of the Son’s existence — not the divine nature itself — that is “emptied.” The debate over harpagmos and pre-existence, in other words, sits inside a wider set of disagreements that are not resolved simply by settling the meaning of one rare Greek word.

sections.title: Colossians 2:9 and Ephesians 3:19 — Whose Fullness?
sections.body:
Paul writes of Christ: “For in him all the fullness of the Deity dwells bodily,” (Colossians 2:9, World English Bible) The Greek noun behind “Deity” is theotēs — an abstract noun for divine essence or being, distinct from the more common theiotēs (“divine quality,” used of God’s general attributes visible in creation at Romans 1:20).

Mainstream commentators — including Peter T. O’Brien, N. T. Wright in his commentary on Colossians, and Murray J. Harris — place weight on Paul’s choice of the stronger, more absolute theotēs rather than theiotēs: the totality of God’s essential being, not merely a derivative divine quality, is said to dwell in Christ “bodily” (sōmatikōs, a word that appears nowhere else in the New Testament), meaning specifically in his incarnate humanity. On this reading, Colossians 2:9 stands among the most direct single statements of Christ’s full deity anywhere in the Pauline letters.

Unitarian and Muslim readers point to a structurally similar phrase used, in the same corpus, of ordinary believers. Paul prays for the Ephesian church: “and to know Christ’s love which surpasses knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God.” (Ephesians 3:19, World English Bible) The same root word for “fullness” (plērōma / plērōthēte) describes believers being filled with the very fullness of God — without anyone concluding that believers thereby become God or members of the Godhead. On this reading, “fullness” language in Paul describes a totality or completeness of God’s presence, action, and revelation, and Colossians 2:9 can be read as saying only that God was present and active in Christ to an unparalleled, unmatched degree — the complete, unlimited outworking of God’s Spirit and authority in him (compare John 3:34, “he doesn’t give the Spirit by measure”) — without asserting that Christ is personally, numerically identical with God.

Mainstream scholars reply that the parallel, while worth taking seriously, is not exact: Ephesians 3:19 is a prayer requesting an ongoing, future, and inherently partial experience (“that you may be filled”) for creatures who remain creatures; Colossians 2:9 is a flat statement of settled fact, using the stronger abstract noun theotēs and the qualifier “bodily,” about the totality (pan, “all”) of deity resting specifically in Christ’s own person. The two texts, on this view, differ in kind rather than merely in the same idea applied at different degrees. The unitarian response is that “fullness” remains, in both cases, a matter of degree along a single continuum — Christ possessing the greatest conceivable degree of God’s presence and authority as God’s unique agent — rather than evidence of a difference in kind between Christ’s relationship to God and any believer’s. Whether the difference between Colossians 2:9 and Ephesians 3:19 is a difference of kind or only of degree is exactly the point on which the two readings divide, and the shared vocabulary of “fullness” does not settle it.

sections.title: Titus 2:13 — The Granville Sharp Rule
sections.body:
Paul writes of the coming hope of believers: “looking for the blessed hope and appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ;” (Titus 2:13, World English Bible) The underlying Greek — tou megalou theou kai sōtēros hēmōn Iēsou Christou — places a single article (tou) before “great God,” with “Savior” joined to it by kai (“and”) and no second article, before the name “Jesus Christ” is appended.

This construction is the classic test case for what is known as the Granville Sharp rule, first stated by the English abolitionist and biblical scholar Granville Sharp in his 1798 Remarks on the Uses of the Definite Article in the Greek Text of the New Testament: when a single article governs two singular, personal, common (non-proper) nouns joined by kai, both nouns refer to the same individual. Since “God” (theou) and “Savior” (sōtēros) in Titus 2:13 are both singular, personal, and common nouns sharing one article, the rule — if it holds — identifies them as one and the same referent, further named as “Jesus Christ”: on this reading, Paul is directly calling Jesus “our great God and Savior.”

Daniel B. Wallace has mounted the most thorough modern defense of Sharp’s rule, searching the corpus of Koine Greek available to him — the New Testament and a wide range of contemporaneous papyri and patristic literature — for constructions meeting Sharp’s stated conditions, and reporting that the pattern holds with very high consistency wherever those conditions are genuinely met. Murray J. Harris, in the same vein, treats Titus 2:13 (together with the nearly identical construction at 2 Peter 1:1, “our God and Savior Jesus Christ”) as among the grammatically strongest individual texts in the New Testament calling Jesus theos outright. It is fair to state plainly that, among the seven passages examined in this article, the grammatical case for a single referent is comparatively strong here — stronger than the disputed points in, for instance, John 1:1 or Philippians 2:6.

That said, the rule has not gone unchallenged. Ezra Abbot, a nineteenth-century Harvard textual critic, produced an extensive dissenting study of Titus 2:13 arguing for a two-person reading (“the great God,” understood as the Father, “and our Savior Jesus Christ”) and proposing counter-examples intended to show Sharp’s rule is not exceptionless; Wallace and other defenders of the rule have in turn argued that Abbot’s proposed counter-examples — several involving plural nouns — do not actually meet Sharp’s original conditions, which apply specifically to singular nouns. The two-person reading has real history behind it: the fourth-century commentator Ambrosiaster, and later Erasmus following him, read the phrase as referring to the Father and the Son separately rather than to Christ alone — a minority position among patristic and Reformation-era interpreters, but not a fabricated one. Modern English translations mostly resolve the ambiguity in favor of the unified reading (the WEB, NIV, ESV, and NASB all render the verse in a way that identifies “our great God and Savior” with “Jesus Christ”), reflecting where the grammatical consensus among specialists — including many who are not defending any particular Christology — presently sits, while a minority of scholars continue to argue the construction can still be read, as Abbot argued, as naming two persons.

sections.title: The Quranic Frame Behind the Unitarian Reading
sections.body:
The unitarian and Muslim readings presented throughout this article do not arise from Greek grammar alone; they sit inside a larger scriptural claim, stated directly in the Quran, about who Jesus was and what he taught. It is worth setting that claim out explicitly, verse by verse, alongside the New Testament passages each corresponds to.

Where John 1:1 calls Jesus “the Word,” the Quran uses exactly that title, addressing Christians directly: “O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.” (Quran 4:171) On this reading, being called God’s “word” marks Jesus as the one through whom God spoke and acted in a singular way — the very sense the qualitative reading of John 1:1 allows — without identifying him as God.

Where John 8:58 raises the question of Jesus’s pre-existence, the Quran addresses his origin directly, comparing him not to God but to Adam: “Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created Him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was.” (Quran 3:59) On the Quranic account, Jesus’s extraordinary origin — created by divine command without a human father — is itself the sign of his status, not evidence of pre-existent deity; Adam, created the same way, is not thereby divine either.

Where John 10:30 and John 17:21-22 raise the question of what kind of “oneness” Jesus shares with God, the Quran records Jesus himself specifying the answer in terms of worship, not shared essence: “They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary" while the Messiah has said, "O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." Indeed, he who associates others with Allah - Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his refuge is the Fire. And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers.” (Quran 5:72) The oneness Jesus calls for, on this reading, is the relational oneness of worshipper and worshipped shared by every servant of God — the same “Lord” for Jesus as for his hearers.

Where John 20:28 raises Thomas’s confession, the Quran responds to any reading of Jesus as divine by pointing to his ordinary humanity: “The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.” (Quran 5:75) Eating food — a mark of bodily dependence understood in Islamic theology as incompatible with God’s necessary, self-sufficient nature — is offered as evidence of Jesus’s createdness alongside his mother’s.

Where Philippians 2:6-8 describes Jesus not grasping at equality with God, the Quran records Jesus using exactly that language of himself, in his first recorded words, spoken in the cradle: “[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.” (Quran 19:30) — servanthood declared outright, rather than equality declined.

Where Colossians 2:9 describes “the fullness of the Deity” dwelling in Christ, the Quran frames Jesus’s exalted status as favor bestowed on a servant, not indwelling identity: “Jesus was not but a servant upon whom We bestowed favor, and We made him an example for the Children of Israel.” (Quran 43:59)

And where Titus 2:13 calls Jesus “our great God and Savior,” the Quran records God questioning Jesus directly on the Day of Judgment about any such claim, together with Jesus’s reply: “And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen.” (Quran 5:116)

None of this settles the Greek-grammatical questions examined above on its own; the Quran is a later text, addressing the question from outside the New Testament corpus, and the case for each unitarian reading given in this article rests on arguments internal to the Greek text and its historical context. But readers should understand plainly that the unitarian readings offered throughout are not free-floating grammatical possibilities invented to counter Trinitarian claims — they cohere with, and are in some cases defended by scholars working explicitly within, this wider scriptural picture of Jesus as God’s supreme created messenger.

sections.title: Weighed Against the Subordination Sayings
sections.body:
Every passage examined above sits inside a Gospel and letter corpus that also records Jesus distinguishing himself from the Father in ways that read, on their face, as plain statements of a lesser status. Two are frequently placed beside the seven divinity texts.

Jesus tells his disciples: “You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.” (John 14:28, World English Bible) And, speaking of the timing of the end of the age: “But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.” (Mark 13:32, World English Bible) — a direct statement that the Son’s knowledge, at least at that moment, was limited in a way the Father’s was not.

Mainstream Trinitarian theology handles both texts through a long-standing distinction, developed particularly from the Cappadocian Fathers of the fourth century onward and formalized at the Council of Chalcedon (451 CE): a distinction between the Son’s equality of essence or being with the Father (the “ontological” or “immanent” Trinity) and a functional ordering within the work of creation and salvation (the “economic” Trinity), in which the Son is sent by, submits to, and — in his incarnate, human nature specifically — experiences real limitation, including of knowledge. On this framework, “the Father is greater than I” describes the Son’s voluntary, relational, and, in the incarnation, human position, not an inferiority of nature; and the ignorance of Mark 13:32 belongs to Christ’s human nature, which was genuinely limited, while his divine nature remains, in classical formulation, omniscient. This is a coherent, carefully worked-out theological system — but it is a system, built to hold two sets of texts together, rather than a conclusion either text states on its own.

Unitarian and Islamic readers apply, in effect, a mirror-image strategy to the seven divinity texts examined above: reading John’s “the Word was God,” Thomas’s “my Lord and my God,” and Paul’s “our great God and Savior” as honorific, functional, or agency language — the kind of exalted language Hebrew and Jewish tradition already applied to specially commissioned agents of God without asserting their literal identity with him: human judges called “gods” in Psalm 82:6; the Davidic king addressed “you are my son” in Psalm 2:7; personified Wisdom present with God at creation in Proverbs 8 — while treating “the Father is greater than I,” “only the Father” knowing the hour, “my God and your God,” and “the only true God” as plain, controlling statements of Jesus’s status as God’s supreme messenger and servant, requiring no auxiliary doctrine to explain away.

Stated honestly, the disagreement at this point is not a matter of counting how many verses sound “high” against how many sound “low.” Both traditions read the full set of texts; neither denies that both kinds of statement exist in the New Testament. The disagreement is over which set of texts is treated as the interpretive key to the other — whether the confessional, worship-directed language (John 1:1, John 20:28, Titus 2:13) should govern how the subordination sayings are read, as expressions of the incarnate Son’s human experience within an already-settled doctrine of his full deity, or whether the subordination sayings should govern how the exalted language is read, as elevated but ultimately honorific speech about a supreme created being who himself calls the Father “greater” and “the only true God.” That is a properly systematic-theological question, resting on judgments about canon, church history, and the nature of scriptural authority that lie beyond what grammar alone can decide — and it is the question that, in the end, separates the two traditions on this subject, not the tally of any particular list of verses.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
A few recurring misunderstandings are worth addressing directly.

First: “Colwell’s rule proves John 1:1 says ‘the Word was God’ in the full Trinitarian sense.” It does not, and careful grammarians on every side of the debate now say so. Colwell’s 1933 study describes a pattern among predicate nouns already known to be definite; it does not, by itself, establish that theos in John 1:1c is definite rather than qualitative, and using it that way reverses the direction of Colwell’s own argument.

Second: “the New World Translation’s ‘a god’ rendering of John 1:1 represents the real scholarly alternative to the Trinitarian reading.” Most specialists, including many who reject a Trinitarian conclusion, regard the fully indefinite “a god” as a weak minority rendering; the scholarship that most seriously challenges the traditional translation is the qualitative reading associated with Harner and the NEB, which says something different from “a god” — that the Word shares God’s nature or quality, a claim compatible with more than one theological conclusion.

Third: “the ‘exclamation’ reading of Thomas’s words in John 20:28 is a common view among biblical scholars.” It is not; this article states plainly that the exclamation and representative readings are minority positions among Johannine scholars, most of whom read Thomas’s words as directly addressed to Jesus within the narrative’s own grammar.

Fourth: “the Granville Sharp rule is a modern invention designed to prove the Trinity.” Sharp’s 1798 study was itself a description of a pattern he found already operating in Koine Greek usage generally, later tested statistically by Daniel Wallace against a wide non-biblical corpus; the debate over Titus 2:13 is a real grammatical dispute carried on by nineteenth-century scholars like Ezra Abbot who had no confessional interest in avoiding the Trinitarian reading, not a modern polemical fabrication on either side.

Fifth: “presenting the unitarian or Muslim reading of these texts means denying that Christians hold their view sincerely, or in good faith.” It does not. Every mainstream Christian reading presented in this article is held by serious scholars working from the biblical text as they understand it; the same is true of every unitarian and Muslim reading presented alongside it.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Which of the seven passages has the strongest grammatical case for calling Jesus God outright?
A: Among the seven, Titus 2:13 is generally regarded as grammatically the strongest single case, because the Granville Sharp construction — one article governing “God” and “Savior,” both joined to the name “Jesus Christ” — has been tested statistically against a wide body of Koine Greek and found to hold with high consistency wherever its conditions are met, according to Daniel B. Wallace’s research. Even here, a minority of scholars, following Ezra Abbot, continue to argue for a two-person reading.

Q: Is Colwell’s rule a reliable proof that John 1:1 calls Jesus “God” rather than “a god”?
A: Not by itself. E. C. Colwell’s 1933 study found that definite predicate nouns preceding the verb in Greek usually lack the article — but it describes nouns already known to be definite from context, and does not independently establish that theos is definite in John 1:1c. Philip Harner’s 1973 study, examining the same construction across the New Testament, argued that most such nouns function qualitatively rather than definitely or indefinitely, a conclusion accepted broadly across the theological spectrum even though its theological implications remain disputed.

Q: Is the “I AM” of John 8:58 definitely a claim to be the God of Exodus 3:14?
A: Mainstream scholarship widely reads it that way, citing the parallel with the Septuagint’s egō eimi ho ōn and the crowd’s attempt to stone Jesus immediately afterward. Unitarian and Muslim scholars respond that egō eimi is ordinary Greek self-identification — used the same way by the healed blind man in John 9:9 — and propose that Jesus is claiming precedence in God’s plan rather than personal, literal pre-existence as God. Both readings are argued in the current literature; the Greek words themselves are used elsewhere for entirely ordinary self-identification, which is why the passage remains genuinely contested rather than settled by vocabulary alone.

Q: Does the Bible ever call Jesus God directly, in so many words?
A: Several passages are argued to do so directly — Titus 2:13, John 1:1, John 20:28, and Colossians 2:9 among them — and each is presented in this article with the mainstream case for that reading. Each of those same passages also has a named, published scholarly alternative reading that stops short of asserting Jesus’s numerical identity with God the Father, and the Gospels also record Jesus calling the Father “the only true God” (John 17:3) and “greater than I” (John 14:28). Which set of texts should control the reading of the others is the systematic question this article closes with, and it is not resolved by any single verse.

Q: Why does this article call the “exclamation” reading of John 20:28 a minority position but not say the same about the qualitative reading of John 1:1?
A: Because that is where the current scholarship actually stands. The qualitative reading of John 1:1, associated with Harner and reflected in the New English Bible, is accepted across the theological spectrum as grammatically well-founded, even though scholars disagree about what it implies theologically. The exclamation and representative readings of John 20:28, by contrast, are held by a small minority of scholars against the clear majority view that Thomas’s words are addressed directly to Jesus. Presenting both as though they carried equal weight in the field would misrepresent the state of Johannine scholarship.

Q: Does presenting both readings mean the question of Jesus’s divinity can’t be answered?
A: This article deliberately does not answer that question, in keeping with its purpose: to lay out the strongest form of each side’s case at the level of the Greek text, named and attributed to real scholarship, so that readers can weigh the systematic arguments — set out in this article’s closing section — for themselves.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below allow readers to go deeper into the primary scholarly arguments summarized in this article, from more than one side of the debate.

Murray J. Harris’s Jesus as God: The New Testament Use of Theos in Reference to Jesus (Baker, 1992) is the standard technical, mainstream defense of reading theos as applied to Jesus in the New Testament, including detailed treatments of John 1:1, John 20:28, and Titus 2:13. N. T. Wright’s The Climax of the Covenant: Christ and the Law in Pauline Theology (T&T Clark, 1991) collects his influential 1986 essay on harpagmos and Philippians 2:5-11, the strongest modern statement of the kenotic reading. James D. G. Dunn’s Christology in the Making: A New Testament Inquiry into the Origins of the Doctrine of the Incarnation (SCM Press, 1980; 2nd ed. 1989) presents the Adam-Christology reading of Philippians 2 and argues more broadly that New Testament belief in Jesus’s pre-existence developed later than the earliest layers of tradition. Anthony F. Buzzard and Charles F. Hunting’s The Doctrine of the Trinity: Christianity’s Self-Inflicted Wound (International Scholars Publications, 1998) is the fullest modern statement of the unitarian case against all seven passages examined in this article, written from within a Christian unitarian tradition rather than an Islamic one, and offered here so readers can weigh its arguments directly.

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌۭ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًۭا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌۭ وَٰحِدٌۭ ۖ سُبْحَٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌۭ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًۭا
quranVerses.translation: O People of the Scripture, do not commit excess in your religion or say about Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary and a soul [created at a command] from Him. So believe in Allah and His messengers. And do not say, "Three"; desist - it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God. Exalted is He above having a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And sufficient is Allah as Disposer of affairs.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ ءَادَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُۥ مِن تُرَابٍۢ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created Him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍۢ
quranVerses.translation: They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary" while the Messiah has said, "O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." Indeed, he who associates others with Allah - Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his refuge is the Fire. And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌۭ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌۭ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْءَايَٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ
quranVerses.translation: The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّۭا
quranVerses.translation: [Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْنَٰهُ مَثَلًۭا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ
quranVerses.translation: Jesus was not but a servant upon whom We bestowed favor, and We made him an example for the Children of Israel.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ
quranVerses.translation: And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
Jesus said to them, “Most certainly, I tell you, before Abraham came into existence, I AM.”
Others were saying, “It is he.” Still others were saying, “He looks like him.” He said, “I am he.”
I and the Father are one.”

that they may all be one; even as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be one in us; that the world may believe that you sent me. The glory which you have given me, I have given to them; that they may be one, even as we are one;
Thomas answered him, “My Lord and my God!”
This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.
Jesus said to her, “Don’t hold me, for I haven’t yet ascended to my Father; but go to my brothers, and tell them, ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.’”

who, existing in the form of God, didn’t consider equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself, becoming obedient to death, yes, the death of the cross.
For in him all the fullness of the Deity dwells bodily,
and to know Christ’s love which surpasses knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God.
looking for the blessed hope and appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ;
You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.
But of that day or that hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.

faq.question: Which of the seven passages has the strongest grammatical case for calling Jesus God outright?
faq.answer: Among the seven, Titus 2:13 is generally regarded as grammatically the strongest single case, because the Granville Sharp construction — one article governing “God” and “Savior,” both joined to the name “Jesus Christ” — has been tested statistically against a wide body of Koine Greek and found to hold with high consistency wherever its conditions are met, according to Daniel B. Wallace’s research. Even here, a minority of scholars, following Ezra Abbot, continue to argue for a two-person reading.

faq.question: Is Colwell’s rule a reliable proof that John 1:1 calls Jesus “God” rather than “a god”?
faq.answer: Not by itself. E. C. Colwell’s 1933 study found that definite predicate nouns preceding the verb in Greek usually lack the article, but it describes nouns already known to be definite from context and does not independently establish that theos is definite in John 1:1c. Philip Harner’s 1973 study argued that most such nouns function qualitatively rather than definitely or indefinitely, a conclusion accepted broadly across the theological spectrum even though its theological implications remain disputed.

faq.question: Is the “I AM” of John 8:58 definitely a claim to be the God of Exodus 3:14?
faq.answer: Mainstream scholarship widely reads it that way, citing the parallel with the Septuagint’s egō eimi ho ōn and the crowd’s attempt to stone Jesus immediately afterward. Unitarian and Muslim scholars respond that egō eimi is ordinary Greek self-identification — used the same way by the healed blind man in John 9:9 — and propose that Jesus is claiming precedence in God’s plan rather than personal, literal pre-existence. Both readings are argued in the current literature, which is why the passage remains genuinely contested rather than settled by vocabulary alone.

faq.question: Does the Bible ever call Jesus God directly, in so many words?
faq.answer: Several passages are argued to do so directly — Titus 2:13, John 1:1, John 20:28, and Colossians 2:9 among them — and each is presented in this article with the mainstream case for that reading. Each also has a named, published scholarly alternative reading that stops short of asserting Jesus’s numerical identity with God the Father, and the Gospels also record Jesus calling the Father “the only true God” (John 17:3) and “greater than I” (John 14:28). Which set of texts should control the reading of the others is the systematic question this article closes with.

faq.question: Why does this article call the “exclamation” reading of John 20:28 a minority position but not say the same about the qualitative reading of John 1:1?
faq.answer: Because that is where the current scholarship actually stands. The qualitative reading of John 1:1, associated with Harner and reflected in the New English Bible, is accepted across the theological spectrum as grammatically well-founded, even though scholars disagree about what it implies theologically. The exclamation and representative readings of John 20:28, by contrast, are held by a small minority of scholars against the clear majority view that Thomas’s words are addressed directly to Jesus.

faq.question: Does presenting both readings mean the question of Jesus’s divinity can’t be answered?
faq.answer: This article deliberately does not answer that question, in keeping with its purpose: to lay out the strongest form of each side’s case at the level of the Greek text, named and attributed to real scholarship, so that readers can weigh the systematic arguments — set out in the article’s closing section — for themselves.

furtherReading.title: Jesus as God: The New Testament Use of Theos in Reference to Jesus
furtherReading.author: Murray J. Harris
furtherReading.note: The standard technical, mainstream defense of reading theos as applied to Jesus in the New Testament, with detailed treatments of John 1:1, John 20:28, and Titus 2:13.

furtherReading.title: The Climax of the Covenant: Christ and the Law in Pauline Theology
furtherReading.author: N. T. Wright
furtherReading.note: Collects Wright's influential 1986 essay on harpagmos and Philippians 2:5-11, the strongest modern statement of the kenotic, pre-existence reading.

furtherReading.title: Christology in the Making: A New Testament Inquiry into the Origins of the Doctrine of the Incarnation
furtherReading.author: James D. G. Dunn
furtherReading.note: Presents the Adam-Christology reading of Philippians 2 and argues that New Testament belief in Jesus's pre-existence developed within, rather than from the start of, the earliest tradition.

furtherReading.title: The Doctrine of the Trinity: Christianity's Self-Inflicted Wound
furtherReading.author: Anthony F. Buzzard and Charles F. Hunting
furtherReading.note: The fullest modern statement of the Christian unitarian case against the seven passages examined in this article, offered so readers can weigh its arguments directly.
